coredata.dc:title: White light emitting lanthanide based carbon quantum dots as toxic Cr (VI) and pH sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Tapas Kumar
Mondal, Supriya
Ghorai, Uttam Kumar
Saha, Shyamal K.
coredata.dc:description: Although, great attention is paid to synthesize fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for versatile applications, the field remains still attractive to achieve white light using these nano materials. In the present work, CQDs are synthesized from citric acid and lanthanide ions viz. Europium (Eu) and Terbium (Tb) are doped in CQD moiety to explore superior optical response for multifunctional applications. By proper tuning of excitation wavelength, perfect white light with Commission Internationale de l’Elcairage (CIE) index (0.345, 0.344) is obtained using these Europium Terbium co-doped CQDs (Eu-Tb-CQD). The observed photoluminescence of white light emitting lanthanide based CQD is pH dependent and will be used as a visual pH sensor. These luminescent Eu and Tb co-doped CQDs are also very useful to detect toxic Cr (VI) with excellent selectivity and sensitivity as compared to pure CQDs. It shows high quenching efficiency (∼95%) in presence of only 160 µM Cr(VI). The selectivity and lower detection limit are also obtained as ∼80% and 0.175 µM respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Co-doped
White light emission
Sensing
FRET process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Europium and Terbium based carbon quantum dots (Eu-Tb-CQD)
Sensing of Cr (VI) in aqueous medium

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CQD; (b) Size distribution histogram of CQD; (c) XRD spectra of CQD; (d) FT-IR spectra of CQD and Eu-Tb-CQD.

(a) Low range XPS spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD; (b) High resolution de-convoluted XPS peaks for C-1s of Eu-Tb-CQD; (c) High resolution de-convoluted XPS peaks for O-1s of Eu-Tb-CQD; (d) High resolution XPS peaks for Tb 3d of Eu-Tb-CQD; (e) High resolution XPS peaks for Eu 3d of Eu-Tb-CQD; (f) High resolution XPS peaks for Tb 4d and Eu 4d of Eu-Tb-CQD.
(a) UV–VIS spectra of CQD, inset shows digital photograph of CQD under visible and UV light; (b) Excitation independent PL spectra of CQD solution.
(a) PL spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD solution; (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of Eu-Tb-CQD; (c) Digital photograph of Eu-Tb-CQD solution at different excitation.

(a) pH effect on PL spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD solution at 365 nm excitation; (b) pH effect on PL spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD solution at 380 nm excitation, inset shows digital photograph of Eu-Tb-CQD at pH14 and pH1; (c) pH reversibility study between pH1 and 12.

(a) PL quenching of Eu-Tb-CQD after gradual addition of different amount of 1 mM Cr(VI) solution; (b) Stern–Volmer plot for Cr(VI) ions of concentration range 1.6 to 160 µM, inset shows linear S-V plot of Cr(VI) ions of lower concentration range from 1 to 20 µM;(c) PL quenching efficiency of Eu-Tb-CQD toward different metal ions; (d) Selectivity of Eu-Tb-CQD towards Cr(VI) ions.
. Synthesis scheme of CQD and Eu-Tb-CQD.
CIE parameter of Eu-Tb-CQD for different excitation wavelength.
Different carbon based quantum dots reported for Cr(VI) detection.
Overlap integral J (λ) values for different metal ions.

head.ce:title: White light emitting lanthanide based carbon quantum dots as toxic Cr (VI) and pH sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science Jadavpur, Kolkata 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science Jadavpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science Jadavpur, Kolkata 700032, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry and Applied Chemistry, Swami Vivekananda Research Center, Ramakrishna Mission Vidyamandira, Belur Math, Howrah 711202, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Industrial Chemistry and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Industrial Chemistry and Applied Chemistry, Swami Vivekananda Research Center, Ramakrishna Mission Vidyamandira, Belur Math, Howrah 711202, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Department of Physics, Government General Degree College at Chapra, Shikra, Padmamala, Nadia 741123, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although, great attention is paid to synthesize fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for versatile applications, the field remains still attractive to achieve white light using these nano materials. In the present work, CQDs are synthesized from citric acid and lanthanide ions viz. Europium (Eu) and Terbium (Tb) are doped in CQD moiety to explore superior optical response for multifunctional applications. By proper tuning of excitation wavelength, perfect white light with Commission Internationale de l’Elcairage (CIE) index (0.345, 0.344) is obtained using these Europium Terbium co-doped CQDs (Eu-Tb-CQD). The observed photoluminescence of white light emitting lanthanide based CQD is pH dependent and will be used as a visual pH sensor. These luminescent Eu and Tb co-doped CQDs are also very useful to detect toxic Cr (VI) with excellent selectivity and sensitivity as compared to pure CQDs. It shows high quenching efficiency (∼95%) in presence of only 160 µM Cr(VI). The selectivity and lower detection limit are also obtained as ∼80% and 0.175 µM respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dot’s (CQD) are a new class of Carbon based fluorescent nano materials with superior properties of stability, eco-friendly, low toxicity, good water solubility. During last 10 years intensive research has been carried out on various carbon based nano materials for numerous applications viz. sensing [1], bio-imaging [2], security ink [3], drug delivery [4], photo catalysis [5] and optoelectronics [6].

In the past few years, significant studies on tuneable optical properties of CQD have been noticed. These optically tuneable properties arise due to several factors like size effect [7–9], surface defect states [10,11] and hetero atom (B, N, S, P) doping [12–15]. CQDs of various emission colours like blue [16,17], green [18], yellow [19], red [20] etc are also well reported. Pang et al. have also synthesized multi color PL emissive carbon dots from blue to red by tuning the reaction temperature and time [21]. Very recently, Yuan et. al have reported triangular CQDs with different emission colors [22]. Ding et al. tune the band gap of CQDs by increasing surface oxidation of carbon dots [23]. CQDs with different colors are also synthesized using same precursor materials in different solvent media [24]. Ghosh et al. prepared white light emitting carbon based quantum dots from graphene oxide by refluxing graphene oxide solution with concentrated HNO3 [25]. Joseph et al. have synthesized white light emitting carbon dots by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite powder [26]. Different materials like Metal Organic Frameworks (MOF) are also used to generate white light for different applications [27]. Recently, study of optical properties of carbon based 2-D materials like graphene oxide or graphite in presence of rare earth metal ions is an interesting topic. Gupta et al. have synthesised red luminescent Europium-complex of graphene nano sheets and studied PL quenching of Rhodamine-B using luminescent graphene [28]. Pang et al. have prepared green luminescent Terbium containing graphene oxide and used to detect hypochlorite in water [29]. Although, intensive research on CQDs with various colors has been carried out during the last few years, tuning of optical properties of carbon based quantum dots in presence of rare earth metal ions with multi functional applications are almost in infant stage.

In the last few years, Carbon based quantum dots are widely used for detection of toxic metal ions and bio molecules such as Hg (II) [30], Pb (II) [31], Cr (VI) [32,33], glucose [34], melamine [35] etc. Chromium (VI) is very carcinogenic metal ion which has several adverse effects on human body. Contamination of Cr (VI) in water occurs from various sources like leather industry, paint industry, metallurgical industry. As per WHO’s (World Health Organization) recommendation, maximum allowed level of Cr(VI) in drinking water is 0.05 mg/L. Different methods like inductively coupled plasma (ICP-MS) [36], Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) [37] X-ray fluorescence (XRF) [38] etc. are used to detect Cr(VI) ions but use of these methods are more complex and expensive. Detection of toxic Cr(VI) using eco-friendly carbon quantum dots based on their fluorescence properties is more effective and easy and as a result, different carbon based quantum dots are used to detect Cr(VI) ions. Zhang et al. prepared cobalt doped carbon dots and used it to detect Cr(VI) ion with detection limit of 1.17 µM [32]. Therefore, carbon dots with high selectivity and sensitivity for detection of Cr (VI) might be a good candidate for detection of toxic Cr(VI).

Here, in this work, we report a simple hydrothermal method for synthesis of pure carbon quantum dots using citric acid as a precursor. These carbon dots show excitation wavelength independent photoluminescence behaviour however tuneable optical properties are achieved after co-doping with lanthanide ions like Eu3+ and Tb3+. These Eu, Tb co-doped CQDs show different PL colours with reddish blue, pure blue, pure white and red at different excitation wavelengths. The CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of white light emitting Eu-Tb-CQD show x = 0.345 and y = 0.344 at 380 nm excitation. These as-synthesized new materials show multifunctional applications in the field of pH sensing, toxic Cr (VI) detection with good selectivity and sensitivity. High efficiency of about 95% PL quenching is obtained only using 160 µM of Cr (VI) solution with high selectivity of about 80% with respect to other metal ions and lower detection limit as 0.175 µM.

Citric acid (>99.0%), Potassium dichromate (>99.5%), Mercury(II) acetate (>98.5%), Cadmium(II) chloride (>98.0%), Chromium(III) chloride hexahydrate (96.0%), copper(II) chloride dehydrate (99.0%), Iron (II) sulphate heptahydrate (>98.5%), Magnesium acetate tetrahydrate (>99.5%), Manganease (II) acetate tetrahydrate (>98.0%), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (>97.0%), lead nitrate (>99.0%), zinc (II) sulphate heptahydrate (>98.0%), Sodium hydroxide (99.0%) are purchased from Merck Life Science pvt. Ltd. Europium (III) chloride (>99.9%), Terbium (III) chloride (>99.99%), Iron (III) chloride (97.0%) and Dialysis bags (1KDa) are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals pvt. Ltd. All reagents are of analytical grade and used without further purification. Milli-Q- water was used for all experiments.

Pure Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were prepared from citric acid as shown in scheme 1 . 0.5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 15 ml of Milli-Q-water and the pH was maintained at 8 using 0.5 N NaOH solutions. The colourless solution was transferred into 60 ml of Teflon equipped stainless-steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 10 h. After completion of the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the solution was filtered through 0.015 µM Millipore filter paper. It was then dialysed through dialysis bag (1KDa) for 24 h to obtain desired CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Europium and Terbium based carbon quantum dots (Eu-Tb-CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize Eu and Tb based CQDs (Eu-Tb-CQD), 12 mg of EuCl3 and 15 mg of TbCl3 were mixed with 5 ml of pure CQD (1 mg/ml) solution. pH of the solution was maintained at 7 by using 0.1 N of NaOH solution followed by addition of 1 mg of tartaric acid to avoid any coagulation. The resulting solution was stirred at 65 °C for 2 h then cooled to room temperature. Again this solution was dialysed through dialysis bag and kept the sample solution in refrigerator.

3 ml of Eu-Tb-CQD solution was taken in a quartz cell and photoluminescence spectra were measured with gradual addition of different amounts of 1 mM Cr(VI) solution at 360 nm excitation. This experiment was carried out at pH7 and also repeated using different metal ions.

3 ml of Eu-Tb-CQD solution was taken in a quartz cell and the pH of the solution was maintained by addition of 0.1 N HCl or 0.1 N NaOH solutions and the final pH of this solution was checked by digital pH meter. PL spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 360 nm at different pH level of Eu-Tb-CQD solution. This experiment was repeated by 3 times for confirmation.

To characterize the as synthesized carbon quantum dots, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was carried out using JEOL-2011 high resolution Transmission Electron Microscope. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were carried out using a NICOLET MAGNA IR 750 system. X-Ray diffraction pattern (XRD) patterns were investigated by the X-ray diffractometer (RICH SEIFERT-XRD 3000P with an X-ray Generator-Cu, 10 kV, 10 mA, and wavelength 1.54 Å). The X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using OMICRON-0571 system. UV–VIS absorption spectra were measured with a Cary UV 5000 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a PTI fluromax QM-400 spectroflurometer. The time resolved photoluminescence measurements were recorded by Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer with the help of time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) apparatus using a 375 nm picosecond diode Laser.

The morphology of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots was investigated by high resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) as shown in Fig. 1 a. It is seen that carbon quantum dots are of almost uniform size with diameter from 5 to 8 nm. The low contrast of CQD arises due to weak change in electron density of CQD and carbon coated copper grid. HRTEM image also reveals that lattice spacing of CQD is ∼0.247 nm and 0.251 nm corresponding to [1 1 2 0] and [1 0 0] planes of graphite (Fig. S1). Fig. 1b shows the size distribution of CQDs to calculate the average size of quantum dots. The Gaussian fitting curve shows that the average size of quantum dots is ∼6.3 nm. TEM image of Eu-Tb-CQD is shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting information).

X-ray diffraction pattern of CQD is shown in Fig. 1c. It shows broad peak centred at ∼17.6° suggesting that carbonisation of citric acid occurs during hydrothermal treatment to produce graphitic structure of carbon dots [39].

Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of as-synthesised carbon quantum dots are shown in Fig. 1d. A broad absorption peak of OH stretching vibration appears at ∼3420 cm−1. A very small peak at ∼2930 cm−1 appears for CH stretching vibration [40] and the peak at 1635 cm−1 corresponds to –CO (COO−) stretching vibration. Two peaks at 1438 cm−1 and 1330 cm−1 appear due to rocking vibration of CH bond [41]. Peaks at 1130 and 1170 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and COC groups. The Peak appears at lower range of 680 cm−1 for aromatic CH bending and 890 cm−1 for CC bond [42]. The FT-IR spectra of Eu-Tb-co doped CQD show similar spectral nature like pure CQD but peaks due to oxygen functionalities (CO and CO) are slightly shifted. Peak appears at ∼662 cm−1 for TbO bond [43] and very small peak at 560 cm−1 corresponds to Eu-O stretching vibration [44].

X-ray photoelectron spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD are shown in Fig. 2 . The full ranges of XPS spectra (Fig. 2a) of Eu-Tb-CQD consist of four elements C (1s at 286 eV), O (1s at 533 eV), Eu (3d and 4d) and Tb (3d and 4d). The high resolution deconvoluted spectra of C1s consist of three different peaks at ∼284.8 eV, 285.7 eV and 288.4 eV for CC, CO and CO bonds respectively as shown in Fig. 2b. Fig. 2c shows the deconvoluted spectra of O1s consisting of peaks at ∼531.2, 532.2 and 533.5 eV for OH, CO and CO bonds respectively. Fig. 2d shows the high resolution Tb 3d spectrum which consists of two peaks at ∼1243.2 eV for Tb 3d5/2 and 1277.7 eV for Tb 3d3/2. Eu 3d spectrum (Fig. 2e) also consists of two peaks at 1134.2 eV for Eu 3d5/2 and 1163.9 eV for 3d3/2. Fig. 2f represents the high resolution spectra for both Eu and Tb 4d core level, in which the peaks appear at ∼141.5 eV and ∼135.1 eV for Eu 4d3/2 and Eu4d5/2 and ∼150.7 eV for Tb 4d respectively. Therefore, XPS and FT-IR spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD confirm the formation of Eu and Tb co-doped CQD.

UV–Vis spectra of pure CQD are shown in Fig. 3 a. CQD shows two absorption peaks at 250 nm and 330 nm which correspond to π → π* transition of CC and n → π* transition of CO respectively. Fig. 3a shown in the inset gives the digital photographs of pure CQD solution under visible light and UV light. Under visible light, CQDs appear as colourless whereas under UV light irradiation at 365 nm these CQDs emit bright blue colour.

Fig. 3b shows the photoluminescence spectra of as synthesized pure CQD under various excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm. It is seen that with increase in excitation wavelength, intensity of emission spectra increases and become maximum intense peak centred at 438 nm when excited at 360 nm. After excitation at 360 nm, emission intensity decreases. But with different excitation wavelengths PL emission peaks do not shift. This excitation independent PL behaviour of CQD indicates similar type of surface states of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots under investigation [40].Thus the present carbon quantum dots may be useful to tune the optical properties by doping of Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions in the carbon moiety.

Optical properties of Eu-Tb co-doped CQD (Eu-Tb-CQD) are shown in Fig. 4 a. It is seen that along with emission peak at 438 nm for CQDs another eight peaks at 490, 545, 594, 616, 621, 651, 685 and 695 nm are appeared due to attachment of Tb3+ and Eu3+ with surface passivating groups of CQDs through oxygen atoms. These peaks are generated mainly due to f → f orbital electronic transition of terbium and europium. Emission peaks appear at 490 nm, 545 nm, 585 nm and 621 nm are corresponding to 5D4 → 7F6, 5D4 → 7F5, 5D4 → 7F4 and 5D4 → 7F3 transitions of Tb ions respectively [29,45] and the emission peaks for Eu are generated due to 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 1, 2, 3, 4) transitions. These peaks are 591 nm (5D0 → 7F1); 615 and 620 nm (5D0 → 7F2); 651 nm (5D0 → 7F3); 685 and 695 nm (5D0 → 7F4) respectively [28,46]. These transitions are guided by selection rule, where the allowed transitions 5D0 → 7F2 (ΔJ = 2) corresponding to electric dipole transition and 5D0 → 7F1 (ΔJ = 1) is magnetic dipole transition [47]. Therefore, incorporation of lanthanides ions into carbon dots makes the material to emit photoluminescence peaks at red region for Eu3+ and green region for Tb3+ ions. With different excitations from 300 to 400 nm, peak positions remain unchanged but peak intensities are changed and Eu-Tb-CQD material shows different emission peaks at blue, green and red regions. At 300, 340, 380 and 400 nm excitations these materials show reddish blue, pure blue, white and red colour respectively. At 380 nm excitation, white light is generated due to proportionate emission peak intensities of different colours. Appearance of white light is also confirmed from the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram as shown in Fig. 4b. It is calculated from the chromaticity parameter (x, y) of PL spectrum. For white light generation of Eu-Tb-CQD at 380 nm excitation, the CIE parameters are obtained as x = 0.345 and y = 0.344. CIE parameters for other excitation are shown in Table 1 . Fig. 4c shows the digital photographs of emission colours of Eu-Tb-CQD with different excitation wavelengths. White light emitting Eu-Tb-CQD shows very good fluorescence stability as shown in Fig. S3 (supporting information). The fluorescence spectra are recorded after different time intervals at 380 nm excitation. It is seen that PL intensity does not change significantly after continuous irradiation under UV light for 4 h. The fluorescence quantum yield of Eu-Tb-CQD has been calculated as 11.42% with respect to quinine sulphate as a reference.

We have compared our white light emitting Eu-Tb-CQD with different reported literature results as shown in Table ST-1 (supporting information). It is seen that quantum yield of as-synthesized white light emitting Eu-Tb-CQD is higher than the literature results and CIE color co-ordinates for white light are (0.345, 0.344) for our Eu-Tb-CQD materials which are almost close to perfect white light color co-ordinates (0.33, 0.33).

Time resolved luminescence decay measurements have been carried out to understand the relative population of excited states of various energy levels. Figs. S4 and S5 show the PL decay of pure CQD and Eu-Tb-CQD. Decay life times and average life times of all the components are shown in Table ST-2 (supporting information). The average life times of pure CQD and Eu-Tb-CQD are 10.05 ns and 6.78 ns respectively. It is seen that due to doping of Eu3+ and Tb3+ average life time of Eu-Tb-CQD decreases by a small amount compare to that of pure CQD. This decrease in PL lifetime arises due to small energy transfer (ET) from QD to lower energy states of Eu3+ (5D0) and Tb3+ ions (5D4). The energy transfer efficiency is 32.5%. Fig. S6a & 6b show both the decay curves of Tb3+ ion with transition 5D4 → 7F5 at emission wavelength 545 nm and for Eu3+ ion with transition 5D0 → 7F2 at wavelength 620 nm. The average life times of Tb3+ ion and Eu3+ ion in Eu-Tb-CQD sample are obtained as 676.9 ns and 1346.5 ns respectively.

We have studied the photoluminescence activity at different pH levels using these white light emitting Eu-Tb-CQD materials. Fluorescence intensities of Eu-Tb-CQD with different pH (pH14-1) at 365 nm and 380 nm excitations are shown in Fig. 5 a and b. It is seen that with decrease in pH from 14 to 1, emission peak of QD (438 nm) decreases gradually and at higher concentration of H+ (pH1) intensity is quenched almost 93%. The surface carboxyl groups of carbon quantum dots are protonated at lower pH level and aggregation of carbon dots occurs due to protonation of surface group resulting fluorescence quenching effect [48,49]. Therefore, intense blue emission of carbon dots is almost quenched at pH1 and yellow emission is obtained due to presence of Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions in CQD moiety. Therefore, at 365 nm excitation, these quantum dots solutions exhibit two distinct colors i.e. blue at higher pH (pH-14) and yellow at lower pH (pH-1) and at 380 nm excitation it shows white luminescent color at pH-14 and yellow color at pH1as shown in the inset of Fig. 5a and b. To check the stability and reversibility of pH detection, we have also carried out this pH sensing experiment up to 10 cycles by continuous changing of pH (pH1 and pH12) of solution. It is seen that PL intensity of solution reversibly changes with pH from pH 1 to pH 14 and shows two different colors (Fig. 5c). Therefore, these Eu-Tb-CQD materials may be useful for application of visible pH sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Cr (VI) in aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para: Another important application of this Eu-Tb-CQD is the sensing of Cr(VI) in aqueous medium. The PL quenching titration experiment was performed after gradual addition of 1 mM Cr(VI) solution to the quantum dots solutions and recorded the photoluminescence spectra under 360 nm excitation as shown in Fig. 6 a. With gradual addition of Cr (VI) solution, all emission peaks are gradually decrease and 95% quenching occurs at only 160 µM concentration of Cr (VI). PL quenching experiment is also performed in presence of Cr(VI) ions at 380 nm excitation. The resulting PL spectra are shown in Fig. S7 (supporting information). Fig. 6b shows the Stern-Volmer plot of this quenching experiment using Stern Volmer equation

I0/I = Ksv [Q] + 1, Where I and I0 are fluorescence intensities in presence and absence of Cr (VI) ion, [Q] is molar concentration of Cr (VI) solution, Ksv is quenching constant. From the inset of Fig. 6b it is seen that in the lower concentration range from 1 to 20 µM, the curve is linear but with increasing concentration of quencher, linearity of S-V curve gets deviated. The calculated value of quenching constant for Cr(VI) is 1.9 × 104 M−1. Detection limit is calculated from linear regression equation from calibration curve shown in the inset of Fig. 6b as I0/I − 1 = 0.019[Cr (VI)] – 0.003 with correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.9994 using the expression 3σ/b, where σ is the standard deviation of blank and b is the slope of the calibration curve. The lower detection limit for Cr(VI) is 0.175 µM which is lower than the maximum concentration of Cr(VI) in the drinking water permitted by WHO [50].

To check the selectivity of Eu-Tb-CQD material toward Cr(VI) ion in presence of other metal ions, we have performed similar quenching experiment using different metal ions. Fig. 6c shows the fluorescence quenching efficiency towards different metal ions. This material shows very weak PL quenching efficiency towards other metal ions (Cr3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Fe2+ etc.) except Cr (VI) ion. It is seen that this material is highly selective to detect Cr (VI) ion with respect to other metal ions and the selectivity is about 80%. This material is also superior to detect Cr (VI) ion in aqueous solution in presence of other metal ions as shown in Fig. 6d. We have also carried out same experiment using pure carbon dots and it shows 59.2% PL quenching in presence of 160 µM Cr (VI). Therefore, presence of lanthanide ions in carbon dots increases the quenching efficiency up to 95% for toxic Cr(VI) detection. Fig. S8 shows the PL spectra for detection of Cr(VI) using pure carbon quantum dots. A comparison between our proposed method using Eu-Tb-CQD materials with some different carbon quantum dots based materials for Cr(VI) detection is also shown in Table 2 . It is seen that our material is very efficient for the detection of Cr(VI) compared to the literature results.

The higher quenching efficiency of this material towards Cr(VI) ion is mainly due to the inner filter effect (IFE). This IFE occurs due to good spectral overlapping occurs between absorption spectra of Cr(VI) solution and excitation spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD materials. Fig. S9 (supporting information) shows the spectral overlap. Here Cr(VI) solution shows broad absorption at 260, 360, 440 nm. Eu-Tb-CQD shows excitation bands in the range from 250 to 500 nm at 438 nm, 545 nm and 620 nm emission. All emission bands of Eu-Tb-CQDs are simultaneously quenched in presence of Cr (VI) due to significant spectral overlapping. Besides this IEF process, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process is also responsible for the PL quenching. Here FRET process also occurs due to effective overlapping between emission band of Eu-Tb-CQD material and absorption band of Cr(VI). Fig. S10 shows the overlapping spectra of different metal ions with emission band of Eu-Tb-CQD material. We also compare values of overlap integrals [J(λ)] of different metal ions in Table 3 . It is seen that Cr(VI) has highest overlap integral as compare to other metal ions and accordingly the value is 3.03 × 1012. Quenching efficiency of white light emitting Eu-Tb-CQD in presence of 160 µL Cr(VI) is 95% whereas in the case of pure CQD the quenching efficiency is only 59.2%. This high quenching efficiency occurs due to inner filter effect and FRET process. For co-doping of Eu and Tb ions in carbon dots moiety, creates additional energy levels near valence band of quantum dots (Fig. S11) through which the charge transfer occurs easily from quantum dots (Eu-Tb-CQD) to Cr(VI) ions. Therefore, as a result of inner filter effect (IEF) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) processes, higher quenching efficiency for Cr(VI) ion detection occurs in the present case.

In summary, we have demonstrated an elegant route to synthesize Eco-friendly pure carbon quantum dots using citric acid as a precursor. These excitation independent CQDs are co-doped with lanthanide ions (Eu3+, Tb3+) to achieve tuneable optical properties and perfect white light with CIE index (0.345, 0.344) is obtained at excitation of 380 nm. These as-synthesized CQDs show multifunctional applications in the field of visual pH sensing and detection of toxic Cr(VI) with excellent selectivity and sensitivity.

The unique feature of these CDQs in the observation of intense white light integrating the emission colors of lanthanide ions (Eu3+, Tb3+) together with the intrinsic emission of CQDs. the PL quenching effect is used for visual pH sensing and toxic Cr(VI) detection. At lower pH level the carboxyl groups of CQDs are protonated and aggregation of CQDs causes the remarkable PL quenching effect. The unique feature of inner filter effect (IEF) and FRET mechanism as arising due to overlapping between the emission spectra of Eu-Tb-CQD and the absorption spectra of Cr(VI) is the origin for this detection of toxic Cr(VI) ions with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. It is also to be noted that our results particularly, the photostability, selectivity and sensitivity of pH sensing and Cr(VI) detection are much better than the literature results. however, there are some scopes to improve the PL intensity, lower detection limit [Cr(VI)] and pH sensing by particle size variation which need to be taken care of in our future study.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: TKM acknowledges CSIR, New Delhi for awarding the fellowships. SM acknowledges Government General Degree College at Chapra, Shikra, Padmamala, Nadia, Govt. of West Bengal for giving permission to continue PhD work. UKG acknowledges Ramakrishna Mission Vidyamandira, Belur math, West Bengal for giving permission for this work. SKS acknowledges DST, New Delhi, Govt. of India, India for providing financial support, project no. SR/S2/CMP-0097/2012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.06.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in graphene quantum dots for sensing
Mater. Today

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sun, L. Wu, W. Wei, X. Qu, Recent advances in graphene quantum dots for sensing. Mater. Today 16 (2013) 433-442

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
